dek: Lake County is really two counties wearing one name — affluent, stable suburbs and post-industrial cities carrying maintenance bills for a population that left — and the index reads it as the pilot's clearest case of inherited wealth going unmaintained.

sections.heading: A serious place, built to last
body: Start with what is genuinely here, because there is a lot of it. Lake County holds roughly 84 properties and districts on the National Register of Historic Places — about 1.7 per 10,000 residents, a deep historic fabric most places would envy. It runs from the 1878 Lake County Courthouse on the Crown Point square to the company town of Marktown to Gary's planned-city neighborhoods like Horace Mann and Morningside, laid out during the steel boom with real ambition. This was a county built to matter, and the bones show it.

The suburbs carry that seriousness forward. Munster, Crown Point, and Lake Central are strong school districts where kids test at or above a full grade of learning per year and above the national average; the county's blended learning rate of 0.95 grades per year is dragged down, not defined, by that suburban strength. Crown Point's courthouse square and the walkable cores of downtown Hammond and Whiting are the real thing. And the natural inheritance is hard to beat: Lake Michigan shoreline, and the Indiana Dunes national and state parks a short drive east, feeding park access that reaches about 11 acres per 1,000 residents. The Lake County Public Library is Indiana's third largest. None of this is nostalgia — it is standing capital, still in use.

sections.heading: Two counties, one median
body: The catch is that almost every county-wide number here is an average of two very different places, and the average hides both. Median household income is about $68,985 and the median home is worth roughly $214,700 — respectable figures that blend suburbs like St. John and Munster, where homes and incomes run far higher, with parts of Gary and East Chicago where values are a fraction of the county line. The same split runs through everything: bachelor's attainment sits at 28.4% county-wide, but that is high in the suburbs and markedly lower in the older industrial cities.

You can watch the divide bend the safety and health numbers too. County-wide violent crime works out to about 5 per 1,000, because most residents live in safe suburbs — but that population-weighted figure sits on top of a rate near 9 per 1,000 in Gary. Life expectancy averages 74.3 years, three to four years below the national mark, with a wide gap between the disinvested urban core and the towns to the south. The county is not one story pretending to be simple. It is two honest stories forced into one column.

sections.heading: The maintenance bill comes due
body: This is where a wealth measure built on stocks, not flows, earns its keep. Cities like Gary and East Chicago lost most of their peak population but kept the streets, water mains, schools, and civic buildings sized for the crowd that left. On the books that reads as a large asset base — net capital assets of roughly $6,500 per resident. In the ground it reads as deferred maintenance: about 22% of roads at poor ride quality, 7.5% of bridges in poor condition against Indiana's 5.3%, and annual capital spending near $180 per resident — too little to hold, let alone renew, what was inherited.

The water tells it most plainly. An estimated two-thirds of Indiana's roughly 55,000 lead service lines — about 36,000 — sit in Northwest Indiana; Gary is replacing some 4,000 and East Chicago still has around 3,400 lead lines clustered near a Superfund site. That is inherited infrastructure whose real value has been falling for decades while its book value held steady. It is exactly the gap a stock-based index is built to see, and it is why Systems, Stewardship, and Health & Safety weigh on the county even where broadband coverage reaches 98% of homes.

sections.heading: The honest decline case
body: A Civic Wealth Index near 35.5 places Lake County last in the pilot — the lowest absolute stock of public wealth of any place profiled here. Its Sovereignty Gap of about -8 is the quieter half of the story: the public wealth, low as it is, still roughly keeps pace with the county's now-modest private wealth. This is not a place where affluence outran the commons. It is a place where both have been worn down together, and where the inherited public stock — the roads, the pipes, the schools in the older cities — is decaying faster than the incomes needed to hold it. That is a real weakness, and the index should name it.

But decline is not ruin, and the assets are not gone. The historic fabric is still standing, the shoreline and dunes are still there, the suburban schools are still strong, and federal money is now paying for catch-up work — lead-line replacement, brownfield cleanup — even as the southern towns keep growing. Lake County's problem is not that it never had civic wealth. It is that it inherited an enormous amount of it and cannot, right now, maintain all of it. Naming that clearly, without erasing what remains, is the whole job of a measure like this one.
